preamble: The shift from the mid front vowel [e] to the high front vowel [i] is a process of Vowel Raising. This can occur as a regular sound change in stressed syllables, part of a chain shift, or as a reduction process in unstressed syllables.

examples.note: Middle English long /e:/ raised to /i:/ as part of the Great Vowel Shift (later diphthongizing in many words).
Raising of /e:/ to /i/ in some Romance verb forms.
Unstressed /e/ reduces and raises towards [i] (phonetically [ɪ]) in Russian.

All Cholan languages (Ch'ol, Ch'orti', Chontal) raised Proto-Mayan long *eː to short /i/. This is one of two parallel raising changes in Cholan (the other being *oː > u), eliminating vowel length in the branch. The change is well-documented in Kaufman and Norman's Proto-Cholan reconstruction and is diagnostic for Cholan membership.

sources: Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction.
Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
Kaufman, T. & Norman, W. (1984). An Outline of Proto-Cholan Phonology, Morphology and Vocabulary. In Munro Edmonson (ed.), Phoneticism in Mayan Hieroglyphic Writing.
